data.title: JFB Construction flags XTEND wins $1.67M Israel defense deal
data.description:
• JFB Construction (JFB) on Tuesday announced that XTEND (XTND) won a $1.67M contract from Israel’s Defense Ministry to supply drone systems.
  • The delivery of these drones is expected to happen in 2026.
  • XTEND (XTND) is already working with defense clients in 30 countries and expects more (and bigger) deals in 2026.
  • This deal strengthens the company’s position in AI-based defense robotics, though exact details are not shared.
  • Moreover, JFB Construction (JFB) and XTEND (XTND) are set to merge in an all-stock deal, and after the deal, the combined company will be listed in the U.S. as XTEND AI Robotics (XTND).
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Eastern Bankshares seen extending dividend growth streak this April
data.description:
Eastern Bankshares (EBC) is anticipated to announce a dividend this April, alongside its first quarter results.

Based on historical trends and Eastern Bankshares’ (EBC) dividend profile, including a 32.69% payout ratio, a 54.02% five-year dividend growth rate, and four consecutive years of dividend increases, analysts expect a consensus annual dividend of $0.54 per share.

That would imply a quarterly payout of $0.135, or a 3.8% increase from the current quarterly distribution of $0.13. The company last raised its dividend last April by 8.3% to $0.13 from a prior dividend of $0.12.

 

 

Eastern Bankshares (EBC) is scheduled to report Q1 results on April 23, 2026.

data.title: NETSTREIT declares $0.22 dividend
data.description:
• NETSTREIT (NTST) declares $0.22/share quarterly dividend, in line with previous.
  • Forward yield 4.21%
  • Payable June 15; for shareholders of record June 1; ex-div June 1.
  • See NTST Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Newmark upsizes credit facility to $900M
data.description:
• Newmark Group (NMRK) said on Tuesday it had revised its senior unsecured revolving credit facility, increasing its size by 50% to $900M and extending the maturity to April 17, 2030.
  • The company had the option to expand the facility up to $1.1B.
  • Borrowings under the credit facility will bear interest, at the company’s option, based on Term SOFR or a base rate, in each case plus an applicable margin of 1.625% or 0.625%, respectively, subject to the company’s credit rating.
  • The facility replaces a $600 million revolving credit facility maturing April 26, 2027, and implies an indicative Term SOFR rate of about 5.27% as of April 17, 2026.
  • The company expects to use its credit facility for general corporate purposes.

data.title: Kratos completes initial flights of upgraded Firejet drone with J85 engine
data.description:
• Kratos Defense & Security Solutions (KTOS) on Tuesday said it has completed initial flight tests of a new version of its Firejet unmanned aerial system equipped with its J85 engine, marking a second major configuration of the platform.
  • The upgraded “Mk1 Firejet” is designed to deliver improved range, endurance, speed and climb rate, while allowing customers to choose between the new model and the existing “Classic Firejet” depending on operational needs.
  • The Classic Firejet, in service since the early 2010s using JetCat engines, is widely used by the U.S. Army’s Target Systems Management Office and allied countries, including Taiwan.
  • Kratos said production of its Spartan engine line, including the J85, is ramping up following the opening of a new facility in late 2025, with output expected to reach thousands of units this year.
  • The company added that the new Firejet configuration can serve both as a target system and as a tactical unmanned aircraft, positioned in the sub-$500,000 category, with capabilities including high subsonic speed, maneuverability, and modular mission systems.
  • KTOS +1.56% premarket to $70.9217.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: World’s leading condom producer plans sharp price hikes as Iran war disrupts supplies
data.description:
The CEO of Malaysia's Karex Bhd, the world's largest condom maker, said on Tuesday that his company will have to raise prices by 20%-30% or even further if supply constraints continue due to the Iran war.

While there are rising freight costs and shipping delays, the company is also witnessing an increase in condom demand, with many of its clients having lower-than-usual stocks, Reuters reported following an interview with Karex CEO Goh Miah Kiat.

"The situation is definitely very fragile, prices are expensive... We ​have no choice but to transfer the costs right now to ⁠the customers," Goh said.

Previously, Karex’s Malaysian peers, including Top Glove (TGLVY) (TPGVF), the world's biggest glove maker, and its rival, Hartalega Holdings (HHBHF), announced plans to raise prices as the Iran war leads to supply constraints for key inputs.

According to Goh, since the onset of war, Karex, a supplier ​to global brands like Durex and Trojan, has seen costs increase across major raw materials, including synthetic rubber and nitrile ‌used ⁠for production, as well as aluminum foils and silicone oil used for packaging.

The company, which produces more than 5B condoms per year, is also facing shipping delays, with deliveries to locations including Europe and the U.S. taking nearly two months compared to a month before the war.

Additionally, the demand for condoms has climbed roughly 30% this year amid sharp declines in stockpiles after significant cuts to foreign aid, especially by the U.S. Agency for International Development, in 2025.

"We're seeing a lot more condoms actually sitting on vessels that have not ⁠arrived ​at their destination but are highly required," Goh said, adding that the company is planning to hike output to meet the rising demand despite having adequate supplies for the next several months.

data.title: Retail sales jump more than expected in March
data.description:
March Retail Sales: +1.7% M/M to $752.1B vs. +1.4% consensus and +0.7% prior (revised from +0.6%), according to advance data released by the U.S. Census Bureau on Tuesday.

Core retail sales (excluding motor vehicles and parts): +1.9% M/M vs. +1.3% consensus and +0.7% prior (revised from +0.5%).

Retail sales, excluding gas and autos: +0.6% M/M vs. +0.2% consensus and +0.6% prior (revised from +0.4%).

Developing… Check back for updates.

data.title: GE Aerospace falls as yearly guidance trails Street view
data.description:
GE Aerospace (GE) beat Wall Street expectations for the first quarter, but shares fell about 3% in premarket trading Tuesday as investors focused on full-year earnings guidance that remained below consensus despite management’s upbeat tone.

GE Aerospace (GE) reported first-quarter revenue of $12.39 billion, ahead of analysts’ consensus estimate of $10.71 billion. Adjusted earnings came in at $1.86 a share, topping estimates of $1.60.

GE Aerospace (GE) maintained its 2026 outlook for adjusted earnings of $7.10 to $7.40 a share and free cash flow of $8.0 billion to $8.4 billion, while saying results are trending toward the high end of the range. Even the top end of the earnings range remained below the Wall Street consensus estimate of $7.46 a share.

The market reaction suggests investors were looking for a formal guidance increase after a strong start to the year, particularly for a stock that has been one of the industrial sector’s top performers. Investors have viewed the company as one of the cleaner industrial growth stories following its breakup from legacy General Electric operations, with a large installed engine base generating recurring high-margin service revenue.

Profit slips on GAAP basis

Net income fell to $1.93 billion, or $1.83 a share, from $1.97 billion, or $1.83 a share. 

Operating profit increased 18% to $2.53 billion, while operating profit margin narrowed to 21.8% from 23.8% a year ago.

Commercial engines drive growth

GE Aerospace (GE) said orders surged 87% to $23 billion in the quarter, helped by strong commercial demand. Commercial Engines & Services revenue climbed 34% to $8.92 billion, while Defense & Propulsion Technologies revenue rose 19% to $3.21 billion.

Chairman and Chief Executive Larry Culp in a statement said, “GE Aerospace had a strong first quarter with orders growing 87% and revenue up 29% supporting double-digit growth in earnings and free cash flow.”

Full-year outlook maintained

GE Aerospace (GE) maintained its 2026 guidance, calling for adjusted earnings of $7.10 to $7.40 a share and free cash flow of $8.0 billion to $8.4 billion, while saying results are trending toward the high end of the range.

For investors, the steady guidance may be as important as the quarterly beat. It signals management still sees enough strength in airline maintenance demand and defense markets to offset concerns about tariffs, slower global growth and geopolitical volatility.

data.title: OpenAI teams up with consulting firms to sell AI coding agent Codex: report
data.description:
OpenAI (OPENAI) is working with several consulting firms, including Accenture (ACN), Capgemini (CAPMF) (CGEMY), and PricewaterhouseCoopers, to help sell its AI coding agent Codex to businesses, the Wall Street Journal reported.

In addition, OpenAI said it reached four million weekly active Codex users, up from three million two weeks ago, the report added. Last week, while providing a major update to Codex, the company said three million developers use Codex every week.

The new Codex consulting program is part of the AI giant's push to focus on coding and enterprise businesses as it cuts back on “side quests," the report noted.

OpenAI, Accenture, Capgemini, and PricewaterhouseCoopers did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

OpenAI faces tough competition from rival Anthropic (ANTHRO) in the AI services market for enterprises. Anthropic has become a dominant AI provider for businesses, in part due to the success of its coding and AI agent products.

OpenAI Chief Revenue Officer Denise Dresser said in an interview that its Codex consulting partners will help the company expand its reach to more potential enterprise customers than it could alone, according to the report.

"Helping companies bridge that gap between how to use it, how to expand it and how to move even more quickly is part of our responsibility, and these partnerships are going to allow us to help scale that to the world," said Dresser.

OpenAI did not specify the financial terms of the partnership program, according to the report.

OpenAI said on Tuesday that it has hired Colleen Kapase as its new vice president of global partnerships and ecosystem.  Kapase will report to Dresser. Kapase, who joins from Google’s cloud unit, will oversee OpenAI’s Codex consulting partners, the report noted.

Besides using Codex to help customers with tasks like updating code, OpenAI's consulting partners will help bring Codex "into every single line of business," said Dresser. That includes areas of knowledge work like marketing, finance, and sales, where coding agents and tools are not currently as prevalent, the report added.

Dresser said Codex and Frontier are aimed at helping automate aspects of all knowledge work.

In February, Microsoft-backed (MSFT) OpenAI announced a deeper push into the lucrative enterprise market with the launch of Frontier, a platform that helps enterprises build, deploy, and manage AI agents. The company signed multi-year partnerships with Accenture, Boston Consulting Group, Capgemini, and McKinsey & Company to help deploy Frontier.

As an example of how Codex can be used outside of software development, Dresser said she has a Codex-built AI agent called "Chief," which summarizes meetings, writes notes, and can update Salesforce and various Slack channels.

OpenAI’s Codex consulting partners will also get access to the AI coding tool under the program. OpenAI already has partnerships with consulting firms such as Bain & Co. and PwC to help sell and use ChatGPT Enterprise, the report noted.

Both Anthropic and OpenAI have used consulting firms to help spread the use of AI and agents across businesses. Anthropic, which has not publicly shared how many people are using Claude Code, said in February that the number of weekly active users for its AI coding assistant had doubled since Jan. 1.

Dresser said OpenAI is also working with its Codex consulting partners to build up Codex Labs, an initiative that brings “hands-on workshops” to businesses to help them start using the coding tool, the report added.

data.title: The future of auto software: Could Rivian be the Android to Tesla's Apple status?
data.description:
A recent article in FT speculated on the potential for Rivian Automotive (RIVN) to license its  electrical architecture and autonomous software. "If you don’t have a high level of autonomy and an increasingly software- and AI-defined vehicle, you’ll lose market share," stated Rivian (RIVN) CEO RJ Scaringe on the future of the auto industry.

UBS analyst Joseph Spak said the idea that the EVs from legacy OEMs could benefit from a common software platform does make sense since it could reduce the cost to make EVs and lower the capital intensity. He said under that scenario vehicles become more homogenized/commoditized from a hardware and base software perspective. "This would then become a bigger test of each individual company’s brand strength to command a premium and/or company scale to generate a profit," he noted. 

Looking ahead, Spak and his team see the potential for an automotive industry that looks more like the smartphone landscape, which is supported by large platforms. That could evolve to Tesla (TSLA) taking on the Apple (AAPL) role and Rivian (RIVN) filling the gap in the Android role.

That leads to the questions of whether Rivian's (RIVN) desire to sell their vehicles becomes a hindrance to the idea of becoming the base architecture/software platform, the role of the Volkswagen (VLKAF) partnership in terms of Rivian (RIVN) licensing its AV software, and the openness of legacy OEMs to enter into AV platform partnerships.

Shares of Rivian (RIVN) were up 1.2% in premarket trading on Tuesday.
